trajectory_summary: This call built energy steadily from a warm but uncertain open to a locked Wednesday appointment. The customer's three consecutive scheduling deflections were the real test of the call, and Tyke navigated all three without losing the booking — a multi-angle resilience win that deserves explicit credit. The structural gaps (wife not included, bill not collected, 'preliminary' framing) are real but lower-risk than they would be on a colder lead given the customer's self-motivated profile and genuine curiosity.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call ended with a booked Wednesday appointment at 10AM. The appointment carries conditional risk — the customer is pre-occupancy, the wife is on title but not included, and the specialist's callback number is unconfirmed — but the booking is real and the customer is self-motivated. Outcome should be tagged: appointment_booked, conditional.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: And then, is there any good is there gonna be anybody else with title with you when you close on Friday?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Tyke asked the right question, got the right answer — wife is on the mortgage and title — and then scheduled the appointment without her. The protocol is explicit: co-title means co-appointment. This is the highest-priority coaching beat because it is a repeatable gap: Tyke clearly knows to ask the question but has not internalized that the answer changes the scheduling move. One sentence at booking closes the gap permanently.

key_moments.quote: I'm moving I'm military, so I'm moving to Virginia. I'm you know, which I have I don't know what utility cost or anything are, but I have read articles how, like, the cost of electricity has skyrocketed, that kind of thing, in Virginia.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer volunteered his motivation unprompted: he has read about Virginia electricity costs and is already framing solar as a potential hedge. This is a bill-pain signal without a bill — the customer is pre-anchored on cost anxiety. Tyke acknowledged it briefly but did not name it back or anchor on it. The emotional alignment-against-utility was available here and was not deployed.

key_moments.quote: And then, is there any good is there gonna be anybody else with title with you when you close on Friday?
key_moments.stewart_read: Tyke asked the title question correctly and confirmed the wife is on the mortgage and title. He then did not include her in the appointment. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct move is to schedule the appointment for a time when both decision-makers can be present. Tyke captured the data and did not act on it.

key_moments.quote: in order to qualify for all the current programs going on right now excuse me. All we ask of the homeowner is that you just have a credit score of at least 670. Is that gonna be an issue for you at all?
key_moments.stewart_read: Tyke stated the credit threshold at 670 — the correct schema value. This is a positive data point given the documented drift in SESSION18_fcbf33c1 where the rep stated 650. Tyke executed the qualifier at the right number, in the right place in the call sequence, without making it feel like an interrogation.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. We can definitely just shoot it for next week then. Again, that's just a preliminary call to make sure all the numbers make sense and just kinda give you a full idea of what it would look like for this home.
key_moments.stewart_read: The strategic read holds here: 'preliminary' was deliberate stakes-lowering appropriate to a pre-occupancy customer who had just given three consecutive scheduling deflections. Tyke read the customer's situation correctly — he is not yet in the home, has no bill, and is making a decision with incomplete information — and framed the appointment as low-commitment to keep the booking alive. This was the right call for this customer at this moment.
